coredata.dc:title: Enhancement in power conversion efficiency of edge-functionalized graphene quantum dot through adatoms for solar cell applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Vaishali
Jha, Prafulla K.
coredata.dc:description:
In search of novel, non-toxic and high performance materials for the use in quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs), we have investigated the effect of adatoms (nitrogen, boron and phosphorus) on carboxyl edge-functionalized graphene quantum dot (COOH-GQD) through the state-of-the-art first principles calculation based on density functional theory. The HOMO, LUMO and energy gaps are analysed in order to check the modulation in electronic properties by the foreign atom through hybrid functional B3LYP with 6-31G basis set. Binding mechanism, molecular electrostatic potential (MESP), and charge transfer are investigated to study the electron injection and charge separation in doped/undoped COOH-GQD. Optical properties show broad spectrum in the visible range favorable to harvest solar light. To envisage the application of adatom doped COOH-GQD in QDSC, the solar cell parameters such as open circuit voltage (Voc), Fill factor (FF), short circuit current density (Jsc) and efficiency (
η )
are presented. The efficiency of COOH-GQD increases by 22–30% after the substitutional doping of nitrogen, boron and phosphorus. Maximum efficiency is achieved in case of phosphorus doping due to its more electron donating nature which will inject more electrons in TiO2 surface. Our findings show that these new sensitizers based on GQD are promising candidates for QDSCs applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Substitutional doping
Graphene quantum dots
Electronic and optical properties
Quantum dot solar cells
Power conversion efficiency

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and solar cell parameters

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representation of photoinduced electron injection from a GQD into a TiO2 surface. An absorbed photon helps an electron from the GQD ground state with energy inside the TiO2 band gap, into an excited state.

Optimized geometries of (a) COOH edge-functionalized GQD (coronene-C24H12), (b) N-GQD and (c) B-GQD (d) P-GQD. Red, yellow, grey, blue, pink, orange balls represent oxygen, hydrogen, carbon, nitrogen, boron and phosphorus respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
HOMO-LUMO plots for doped/undoped COOH edge-functionalized GQDs.
MESP plots for doped/undoped COOH edge-functionalized GQDs.
Mulliken plots for doped/undoped COOH edge-functionalized GQD.
UV–vis for doped COOH-GQD. Inset figure shows the UV–vis for COOH-GQD.
Calculated J-V curve of doped/undoped COOH-GQD at T = 300 K.
Calculated solar cell parameters of considered systems along with HOMO, LUMO energies, HOMO-LUMO gap (Eg), electron affinity (EA) and work function (WF).

Calculated maximum absorption wavelength,
λ
max
(nm), electronic transition energy (eV), oscillator strength (f) and light harvesting energy (LHE) of all considered systems.
Calculated solar cell parameters of all considered systems.

head.ce:title: Enhancement in power conversion efficiency of edge-functionalized graphene quantum dot through adatoms for solar cell applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, The M. S. University of Baroda, Vadodara, Gujarat, 390002, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The M. S. University of Baroda

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In search of novel, non-toxic and high performance materials for the use in quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs), we have investigated the effect of adatoms (nitrogen, boron and phosphorus) on carboxyl edge-functionalized graphene quantum dot (COOH-GQD) through the state-of-the-art first principles calculation based on density functional theory. The HOMO, LUMO and energy gaps are analysed in order to check the modulation in electronic properties by the foreign atom through hybrid functional B3LYP with 6-31G basis set. Binding mechanism, molecular electrostatic potential (MESP), and charge transfer are investigated to study the electron injection and charge separation in doped/undoped COOH-GQD. Optical properties show broad spectrum in the visible range favorable to harvest solar light. To envisage the application of adatom doped COOH-GQD in QDSC, the solar cell parameters such as open circuit voltage (Voc), Fill factor (FF), short circuit current density (Jsc) and efficiency (
η )
are presented. The efficiency of COOH-GQD increases by 22–30% after the substitutional doping of nitrogen, boron and phosphorus. Maximum efficiency is achieved in case of phosphorus doping due to its more electron donating nature which will inject more electrons in TiO2 surface. Our findings show that these new sensitizers based on GQD are promising candidates for QDSCs applications.

ce:section.ce:para: To satisfy the world's gigantic need for clean energy, third generation photovoltaic (PV) is becoming the only feasible solution due to its low cost efficiency and electricity generation on earth [1]. The quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs) are arising as a prominent new type of low-cost solar cells due to their extraordinary properties like shape-size tunable gaps, high absorption coefficient etc. [2,3]. Colloidal lead sulfide (PbS), cesium lead iodide (CsPbI3), CdSe and CdTe quantum dots based solar cells are extensively used as QDSCs due to their size-dependent optical properties and potential in surpassing the Shockley-Queisser limit [4–6]. Despite high efficiencies acquired by these QDSCs, they consist of highly toxic metals like lead, cadmium etc. Accordingly low-toxicity, biocompatible and environment-friendly substitutes are in urgent requirement for the fabrication of solar cells. In order to explore significant characteristics of QDSCs, three device compositions were considered: (a) PV effect arising between metal-QD junction, which are named as Schottky junction solar cells, (b) PV effect arising between QD-polymer junction, namely organic solar cells and (c) Quantum dot solar cells (QDSCs) which works on a principle that the photovoltaic (PV) effect takes place among quantum dot combined with metal oxide semiconductor (with wide bandgap) and a redox electrolyte [7–12]. Apparently, they are somehow similar to the dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs), with the only difference that PV effect takes place between dye-metal oxide semiconductor and redox electrolyte and therefore attracted particular attention for the consideration in solar cells.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero dimensional member of the graphene family have been explored greatly due to their unique and significant energy, optoelectronic, light emission and bio-imaging applications [13]. They have attracted so much attention in research society for the utilization in dye sensitized solar cells primarily on account of their nontoxic, highly stable, biocompatible characteristics [13]. Due to the quantum confinement, edge effects, size dependent band gap tailoring and remarkable light harvester they are considered to be well adapted for solar cell applications. For instance, various kinds of carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots based solar cells have been reported [14]. Up till now, GQDs are used as hole transport layer (HTL) material, semiconductor/GQDs and heterojunction solar cell. Zhang et al. [15] obtained the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 0.13% for nitrogen doped carbon dots. Recently, Wang et al. [16] designed nitrogen doped carbon dots solar cell with the PCE of 0.79%. Moreover, the power conversion efficiency of QDSCs is yet to be improved. The influence of four heteroatoms on electronic and optical properties of GQDs has been recently investigated [17]. Edge functionalized graphene quantum dots with oxygen presents significant non-zero bandgap and luminescence on excitation [18,19]. Polyaromatic hydrocarbons (PAH) are often considered as an ideal for GQDs [20,21]. It is because of the fused hexagonal rings of PAH that are regarded as small fragments of graphene sheet where all the dangling bonds are passivated through hydrogen atoms [22–26]. Motivated by the above facts, herein, we investigated the electronic properties and solar cell parameters for nitrogen, boron and phosphorous doped carboxyl functionalized GQDs using first principles study based on density functional theory (DFT). Our design method consists of replacing one carbon atom of carboxyl functionalized GQD through nitrogen, boron and phosphorous individually in order to improve its structural properties, electronic properties, optical absorption along with its performance in QDSCs applications.

In present paper, DFT calculations are performed to investigate the doping effect of three adatoms namely boron (B), nitrogen (N) and phosphorous (P) with COOH-GQD to be used in QDSCs. Geometry optimization of doped and undoped GQD is done using hybrid functional B3LYP with 6-31G basis set in the Gaussian09 suite of program [27–29]. Vibrational frequencies are also calculated to check whether structures are minima with no imaginary frequencies. Moreover, to attain the accuracy in the protocol, we further re-optimized the structures for single point calculation at same level of theory in both doped/undoped systems. The highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) and band gap (Eg) are visualized using GaussView software. After optimization, we have performed time dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations in the framework of integral equation formalism model (IEFPCM), to study the electronic transitions and UV–vis absorption spectra. The solvent effect in calculation is considered using self‐consistent reaction field (SCRF) method through the Polarizable Continuum Model (PCM). PCM model executes a reaction field calculation using the integral equation formalism model (IEFPCM) [30]. IEFPCM model generates the solute cavity through an array of overlapping spheres. The solvent effects are described in terms of macroscopic properties of solvents like dielectric constant (ε), surface tension etc. at 298 K. In our case, the solvent is water for which ε is 78.3. Also the polarization of chosen solvent is expressed in terms of surface charge density on the cavity surface and produces electric field in the cavity making an effect on solute [31–33]. We have calculated UV–vis with the inclusion of water as a solvent. For implication of doped/undoped GQDs to the QDSCs, we have calculated solar cell parameters such as open circuit voltage (Voc), Fill factor (FF), short circuit current density (Jsc) and efficiency (
η )
. The solar cell efficiency (
η
) is calculated by [34]:
(1)
η =
F F .
J
s c
.
V
o c
P
i n

The other parameters like
V
o c
, F F
and
theoretical
J
s c
are calculated using equations [35–37]:
(2)
V
o c
=
E
L U M O
G Q D s
−
E
C B
T i
O
2

The open-circuit voltage,
V
oc
, is defined as the maximum voltage from a solar cell which takes place at zero current. The
V
o c
which is given through eq. (2) is the energy difference between LUMO of donor (GQDs) and conduction band of acceptor TiO2. The
V
o c
relies upon the saturation current and light-generated current, also it measures the amount of recombination in device.
(3)
F F =
ϑ
o c
− ln
(
ϑ

+ 0.72 )

+ 1
;

q ×
V

K
B
T

Fill factor is generally known through its abbreviation “FF” which evaluates the maximum power from a designed solar cell device and is associated with
V
oc
and
I
sc
.
I
s c
is short-circuit current. FF can be demonstrated through eq. (3) in which
ϑ
o c
is the normalized
V
o c
[38]. LHE is the key parameter to improve Jsc to identify efficiency of QDSCs. The greater LHE will lead to the larger efficiency. LHE or absorptance (A) is the fraction of light intensity absorbed at a particular wavelength in DSSCs or QDSCs [39].
(4)
L H E = A =
I
a b s
I
o

Here
I
a b s
and
I
o
is the absorbed and incident intensity respectively. Further,
L H E
is proportional to the incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) and is evaluated through the expression [40]:
(5)
I P C E =
(
L H E
)
φ
η
c
= A φ
η

Where,
η
c
is the charge collection efficiency through photoanode and
φ
is the quantum yield of charge injection. Through the above magnitude, it is possible to achieve at an expression to calculate the short-circuit current density (
J
s c
) [41],
(6)
J

=
∫
q
A φ
η
c
(
E
)
I
s u n

d E

Here q be the electron charge, and
I
s u n
(E) is the photon flux density taken from AM1.5G spectrum. In our case, φ and
η
c
are weakly dependent on wavelength (λ), therefore, equation (6) can be written as an expression [41]:
(7)
J
s c
=
∫
0
∞
q
A
(
E
)
I
s u n
(
E
)
d E

The
J
s c
is the current density when the voltage over the solar cell is zero and is given by eq. (7). The parameter
J
s c
depends on various factors like solar cell area, spectrum of incident light, material's optical properties etc. In the present calculation, we consider
E
C B
T i
O
2
= −4.0 eV [42,43].

In order to understand the rationality of our calculations, we first analysed the structure and properties of graphene quantum dot (GQD) before and after the substitutional doping of nitrogen, boron and phosphorous. An electron donor coronene molecule (C24H12) was modelled as a GQD with the edges terminated by two carboxyl groups, naming it as “COOH-GQD”. Fig. 1 presents the schematic of GQD with TiO2. Fig. 1 depicts the photoinduced transfer of electrons from donor GQD to the acceptor TiO2 surface. The boost in electron from ground state of GQD to the excited state i.e. the conduction band of TiO2 is the result by an absorbed photon. Through various functioning (adiabatically or non-adiabatically), this excited electron is then injected inside the conduction band of TiO2. Fig. 2 presents the optimized structures of carboxyl (COOH) edge-functionalized GQD (coronene) along with the three substitutional adatoms. The optimized bond length between the carbon atoms in COOH-GQD is ∼1.42 Å. In the figure, C, H, O, B, N and P represents the carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, boron, nitrogen and phosphorus respectively. Commonly, in benzene rings which are attached to carboxyl groups, the carbon-carbon bond (C-C) ranges from 1.48 to 1.50 Å and carbon-oxygen (C-O) ranges from 1.21 to 1.215 Å [44]. Our calculated bond-length of C26-C35 is 1.48 Å and C35-O37 (O38) is 1.24 Å. Here, C26 and C35 are the 26th and 35th number of carbon respectively (Fig. 2).

Further, the optimized bond angle of C13-C14-C36 is 122.06°. However, the angle is increased by 2.06° due to the repulsion in carboxyl group and benzene ring present in GQD. From the figure we can clearly see the effect of substitutional doping in COOH-GQD. Upon the doping of nitrogen atom (Fig. 2 (b)) in COOH-GQD, the bond length of C-C atom changes from 1.42 Å to 1.40, 1.43 and 1.45 Å in N42-C8, N42-C41 and N42-C22 respectively (numbering can be seen from Fig. 2). Similarly, on doping of boron atom (Fig. 2 (c)), the bond distance increases to 1.505, 1.508, 1.54 Å in B42-C8, B42-C41 and B42-C22. Our calculated bond distance between carbon-nitrogen and carbon-boron atoms matches with the reported work [45]. The less difference in the bond distances between carbon, nitrogen and boron can be attributed to the similar atomic radii of these elements. Further, the doping of phosphorus in COOH-GQD (Fig. 2(d)) distorts the hexagonal ring reaching the bond length up to ∼1.7 Å for C-P atoms. This change in structure of GQD can be attributed to the fact that the inclusion of these three adatoms lead to the disruption in sp2 hybridization of the carbon atoms, and would cause significant changes to the electronic properties of COOH-GQD. In addition, we have calculated the binding energies of COOH-GQD and doped COOH-GQD to compare the stabilities of different structures. The binding energies of doped/undoped COOH-GQD are calculated as:

For COOH-GQD
(8)
E
b
=
[
nE
(
C
)
+ nE
(
H
)
+
nE
(
O
)
− E
( COOH − GQD )
]
42

For nitrogen/boron/phosphorus doped COOH-GQD
(9)
E
b
=
[
nE
(
C
)
+ nE
(
H
)
+
nE
(
O
)
+ nE
(
B / N / P
)
− E
( B / N / P − COOH − GQD )
]
42

Where
E
(
C
)
,
E
(
H
)
and
E
(
O
)
are the energies of one atom of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen respectively.
E
( COOH − GQD )
and
E
( B / N / P − COOH − GQD )
are the total energy of the doped/undoped systems and
n
is the number of atoms in all considered systems. Table 1 presents the binding energies of doped and undoped COOH-GQD. The binding energy per atom for COOH-GQD is 4.3 eV, which further increases after the doping of boron, nitrogen and phosphorus.

All calculated binding energies presented in Table 1 are positive confirming stability of all structures. However, the similar binding energies (Eb) of all doped systems can be explained in terms of their structural properties. The Eb is similar due to their almost similar ionic radii of the dopants with nominal change in bond-lengths between C-N and C-B. However, in case of P doping the slight distortion in hexagonal ring changes bond-length from ∼1.42 to 1.7 Å resulting in lower binding energy as compared to other (Eb = 6.72 eV). Table 1 also presents the total dipole moment for all doped/undoped COOH-GQD.

The doping of boron, nitrogen and phosphorus to COOH-GQD increases the total dipole moment. Phosphorus doped COOH-GQD has the highest dipole moment of 3.16 Debye, which can be attributed to the structure change, generating difference between local dipoles. The increase in dipole moment of phosphorous doped GQD can be attributed to the distortion of hexagonal structure adjacent to phosphorus atom in COOH-GQD. Addition of P atom in COOH-GQD results in the broken symmetry in π-electrons of GQD which in turn leads to the increased dipole moment as compared to others. The rise in dipole moment under mechanical contortion is due to broken symmetry. Also the different values of electronegativity of carbon and phosphorus results in charge redistribution and contribute in formation of dipole moment [46].

Table 1 presents the calculated HOMO-LUMO energies, HOMO-LUMO gap (Eg) for all considered systems. The influence of adatoms on electronic properties can be seen from the change in HOMO-LUMO energy values represented in Table 1 and Fig. 3 . A clear difference in the HOMO, LUMO energy levels and Eg can be seen from the calculated values in Table 1. The doping of adatoms slightly reduces the band gap. The order of band gap is P-GQD (3.34 eV) < N-GQD (3.38 eV) < B-GQD (3.41 eV) < COOH-GQD (3.47 eV). From Fig. 3, it is found that the LUMO energies of all doped/undoped COOH-GQDs are greater than the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 (−4.0 eV) [42] which shows that the electrons may inject from the excited states of the doped/undoped systems to CB of TiO2 efficiently. Furthermore, the HOMO energies are lower than the redox couple of I−/I3 − (−4.8 eV) [47] which demonstrates that the regeneration process may be rapid and effective.

In order to understand the relation between structure and activity of the molecule, we have calculated the molecular electrostatic potential (MESP). The MESP can be defined as the potential experience by a unit positive charge in the vicinity of the molecule due to their electron density distribution. MESP shows the chemical reactivity as positive potential can predict the sites for electrophilic attack whereas the negative potential shows sites for nucleophillic attack. The MESP at a point r in the surrounding of a molecule (in atomic units) can be calculated from equation [48,49],
(10)
V
(
r
)
=
∑
Z
A
|
R
A
− r
|
−
∫
ρ
(
r
'
)
dr
'
|
r
'
− r
|

Where
Z
A
is the charge on nucleus A positioned at
R
A
and
ρ
(
r
'
)
is the density at any point r. V(r) describes the net electrostatic effect created at the point r by the total charge distribution (electrons + nuclei) of the molecule.

Fig. 4 shows the MESP of doped and undoped COOH-GQD in the plane of the base ring. The MESP of all considered systems are mapped on the surface of electron density of 0.002 unit. The red colour surface in the figure shows the negative electrostatic potential while the blue colour surface shows positive electrostatic potential. The potential in MESP is in the order of red < orange < yellow < green < blue. The green surface shows the zero potential region. After the inclusion of boron and phosphorus, the carboxyl group graphene quantum dot (coronene) tends to show more negative electrostatic potential making them better candidate for electron injection.

Further to study the atomic charge distribution of COOH-GQD and N, B, P doped COOH-GQD, we have calculated the Mulliken population analysis (MPA) at the same level of theory. Fig. 5 shows the Mulliken plots of doped/undoped COOH-GQD. From the plot (Fig. 5 (a)), we can see that the carbon and oxygen carry both positive and negative charges while hydrogen carry only positive charges, shown in several reported works [50–52]. The incorporation of adatoms (N, B and P) clearly indicates the charge transfer from different hydrogen and oxygen atoms nearby the dopants. Nitrogen shows the negative charge however, boron and phosphorus shows positive charges, transferring charges to carbon. Phosphorus shows large positive charge among all which can be attributed to the donor nature of phosphorus as it consists of five valence electrons, but on the third electron shell, producing a significant larger covalent atomic radius (1.06 Å, compared to 0.82 Å for B, 0.77 Å for C, and 0.75 Å for N) [53–55].

To further check the effect of doping on the electronic properties of COOH-GQD, we have calculated the work function of doped/undoped COOH-GQD. Work function is the minimum amount of energy required by an electron present in the highest filled level to jump outside the solid at field free zone at absolute temperature and can be calculated through the following equation [56]:
(11)
W F =
( I E + E A )
/ 2

Here ionization energy (IE) and electron affinity (EA) are expressed by HOMO-LUMO orbital energies obtained through Gaussian09 package [29] as IE = -HOMO and EA = -LUMO. The WF for COOH-GQD shows a significant change after doping of elements. The
W F
of undoped COOH-GQD is calculated as 4.11 eV, which remain nearly same after the doping of N and it decreases with doping of B. A distinct behaviour has been seen with the doping of P as it significantly increases the
W F
of COOH-GQD. The calculated work functions are shown in Table 1. The change in work function explains the transfer of charge between COOH-GQD and the dopants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and solar cell parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 presents the UV–vis spectra for all doped and undoped COOH-GQD at ten excited states with water using same level of theory. In order to harvest more photon, the absorption spectra of all donors (COOH-GQD, N/B/P-COOH-GQD) should be analogues to solar spectrum. For all considered systems, we have observed that all the absorption exist in the visible region. The nature and energy of electronic transition are studied through the main singlet-singlet electron transition with the oscillator strength [57]. Oscillator strength presented in Table 2 reveals the possibilities of absorption and emission in transition between HOMO-LUMO energy levels [58].

For the highest peak wavelength and oscillator strength, the major contributions of electronic transition are due to H-1→LUMO (12%), HOMO→L+1 (78%) and minor contributions of electronic transition are due to H-2→L+2 (3%), H-1→L+1 (2%) in our closed shell COOH-GQD [13]. It is clear from Fig. 6 that doping of N, B and P can alter the absorption properties of COOH-GQD. Fig. 6 shows a broad UV–vis peak and shoulder at 316 nm and 400 nm respectively. This result is comparable with the available experimental work [59,60]. The slight shift from the reported experimental work is due to the various factors like unknown concentration of carboxyl group attached at the edges of GQD, size and shape. As GQDs are known to have highly size and shape dependent electronic and optical properties, the optical properties will vary resulting in shift of the UV–vis peak [61]. This transition can be attributed to the n→π* transition due to CO. The peaks due to doping of nitrogen, boron and phosphorus is red shifted with the order COOH-GQD (361 nm) < B-COOH-GQD (440 nm) < N-COOH-GQD (444 nm) < P-COOH-GQD (456 nm) which is consistent with earlier theoretical result [17]. This can be due to the relatively large radius and extra valence electrons in N and P atoms, generating the modification of the electronic transition properties of COOH-GQDs. Phosphorus doped COOH-GQD among all shows the strongest absorption at 456 nm indicating more light harvesting at longer wavelength [62]. This in turn, further increase the power conversion efficiency of phosphorus doped COOH-GQD solar cells. The optical band gap for COOH-GQD with and without substitutional doping evaluated by Tauc plot are shown in Fig. 7 . The evaluation of the optical band gap using UV–vis absorbance spectra is determined using the Tauc relation, as given in equation [63]:
(12)
αhν ≈
( hν −
E
g
)
n
Where
α
is the absorption coefficient,
E
g
is the energy gap, and
hν
is the photon energy.

Fig. 7 shows the (
αhν )
2 verses
hν
of direct allowed transition of doped and undoped COOH-GQD, where the direct band gap transition would result in a more efficient absorption of solar energy [64]. The optical band gap for COOH-GQD is 3.51 eV, which further reduces to 2.04, 2.15 eV and 2.09 eV upon the doping of nitrogen, boron and phosphorus respectively. Our calculated optical band gaps show good agreement with other reported works [59,60]. The reduction of optical bandgap (∼2eV for all considered systems) will result in the application of graphene quantum dots in optoelectronics.

For QDSCs to be used in solar cell applications, we have evaluated solar cell performance parameters such as open circuit voltage (Voc), short circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF) and efficiency (
η
) of both doped and undoped COOH-GQD. With the motivation to achieve low-cost and highly efficient photovoltaic devices for solar cell applications, TiO2 photoanode has been of great importance. Among all metal oxides that are used in QDSCs, TiO2 plays a significant role considering their charge collector behaviour [65,66]. Apart from that TiO2 is economical, abundant in nature and environment-friendly. The synthesis of GQDs on TiO2 results in the active charge injection from graphene quantum dots to TiO2 [67]. Further, GQDs being π-electron systems can be placed in direct connection with TiO2, accelerating the donor-acceptor junction. It has been found that the electronic coupling between GQD and TiO2 can be intensified through different functional groups [67]. In our case, the COOH-GQD (donor) will link to TiO2 surface through COOH functional group attached at the edges, producing high injection of electrons. Additionally, through the analysis of electronic properties of doped/undoped COOH-GQDs, we found that the LUMO energies of all doped/undoped systems are higher than conduction band of TiO2 depicting the faster electronic injection from exited states of GQDs to conduction band of TiO2. The HOMO energies are under redox couple of I−/I3 − indicating quick and efficient regeneration process. There exist several reports regarding the nanoscale carbon materials (graphene, CNT, GQDs etc.) with TiO2 due to their capabilities of harvesting broad spectrum of solar light and fast charge separation [67–69]. The different electronic levels of doped/undoped COOH-GQD, allows hot electron injection and significant charge separation on TiO2. For the analysis of results to be used in quantum dot solar cell applications, we considered TiO2 nanocrystalline surface due to the fact that the LUMO energies of both doped/undoped COOH-GQDs are above the conduction band of TiO2 and the HOMO energies of doped and undoped COOH-GQDs are beneath the redox couple of I−/I3 − [47]. The fast electron injection of doped/undoped COOH-GQDs onto TiO2 semiconductor will play an important role in increasing the efficiency of QDSCs. Table 2 presents the maximum absorption wavelength (
λ
max
), electronic transition energy, oscillator strength (f) and light harvesting energy (LHE) of all considered systems. The LHE can be calculated from the following equation [70]:
(13)
L H E = 1 −
10
− f
where
f
is the oscillator strength obtained through TD-DFT calculations. The order of LHE of all systems are COOH-GQD (0.83) > P-COOH-GQD (0.21) > B-COOH-GQD (0.183) > N-COOH-GQD (0.16). There is only slight difference in LHE for N, B, and P doped systems which will result in the comparable photocurrents in solar cell. Table 3 presents the calculated solar cell parameters for doped/undoped COOH-GQD.

The Voc is an essential PV parameter that can be determined theoretically by the difference between the LUMO of the donor and the conduction band of TiO2 [7]. The Voc of COOH-GQD depends on the LUMO levels of their donor counterparts (doped with N, B and P). The Voc of our all considered systems ranges from 1.39 to 1.63 eV. The positive values of Voc show easy electron transfer from the doped COOH-GQD to TiO2 [7].

We have plotted the calculated light J-V curve in Fig. 8 of all doped/undoped COOH-GQD and found the highest Jsc in P-COOH-GQD and lowest in undoped COOH-GQD. The total current density is evaluated through following equation [71]:
(14)
J =
J
s c
−
J
0
(
e
q V
k T
− 1
)

Where
J
0
is the saturation current density which is the sum of all recombination mechanisms comprising current density of the Shockley-Read-Hall (SRH), surface, contact, and Auger recombination mechanisms [72]. The undoped COOH-GQD has a Jsc of 0.47 mA/cm2, a FF of 0.91 and efficiency of 0.7%. The efficiency further increases to 0.86% (≈22.8% enhancement) upon the doping of nitrogen which is higher than the reported efficiency in n-doped CDs [16]. Moreover, the doping of phosphorus enhances the efficiency up to 0.909% (≈30% enhancement) which can be attributed to their more electron injection on TiO2 surface. Our results suggest that the phosphorus doped COOH-GQD is most promising candidate to be used in solar cell applications.

In summary, we theoretically calculated the effect of substitutional doping on power conversion efficiency of doped and undoped COOH edge-functionalized GQDs. Our study reveals that the efficiency of COOH edge-functionalized GQD shows prominent enhancement after doping of nitrogen, boron and phosphorus atoms as they typically changes the electronic and optical properties. The doping also shows change in frontier molecular orbital energy levels and energy band gap of all considered systems leading to the change in their photoelectric properties. The energy gap of COOH-GQD, N-COOH-GQD, B-COOH-GQD and P-COOH-GQD calculated at DFT/B3LYP/6-31G level are 3.47 eV, 3.38 eV, 3.41 eV and 3.34 eV respectively. The binding mechanism provides significant guidelines for the design in high performance solar cells. The strongest absorption at 450 nm for phosphorus doped COOH-GQD suggests more light harvesting at longer wavelength for this system. The reduction of optical bandgap will result in the application of GQD in optoelectronics. The calculated values of Voc corresponding to TiO2 of doped/undoped COOH-GQD are efficient for a possible electron injection from the donor to the acceptor lying. P-COOH-GQD has the highest value of short-circuit current density (Jsc) attributed to their small band gap and high HOMO energy levels as compared to all other considered doped systems further affecting the photoelectric properties. The trend in the increment of efficiency is P-COOH-GQD > B-COOH-GQD > N-COOH-GQD > COOH-GQD. The efficiency of P-COOH-GQD is maximum due to the high electron injection in TiO2. Our theoretical calculations show that by adding a foreign atom in edge-functionalized GQD makes it a suitable candidate for next generation highly efficient quantum dot solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to DST for providing in terms of Indo-Poland (DST/INT/POL/P-33/2016) project for financial assistance.
